design_philosophy.core_loop: Collect resources (gold/wood) → Build economy (farms, town hall) → Construct army (barracks, magical buildings) → Defeat opponent forces through real-time tactical combat
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Balancing resource scarcity and unit production speed against opponent expansion and military pressure; managing limited supply caps through farm construction
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Command either the noble Humans or invading Orcs to build civilizations and dominate enemy forces through strategic base management and tactical combat
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Expands the Dune II paradigm with diverse mission types beyond 'destroy all enemies' (rescue missions, limited-force scenarios, rebel suppression). Emphasizes skillful management of relatively small, asymmetric forces rather than massive armies. Introduced multiplayer as essential to RTS design. Each faction has identical building functions but different visual identity and spell/unit abilities, creating strategic asymmetry

difficulty.checkpointing: Mission-based checkpoints; single-player campaigns progress through sequential missions with varied objectives
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Mission variety provides organic difficulty scaling; players manage their own economy and army composition; limited-force missions constrain agency to efficient unit utilization only

progression.upgrade_system: Spell research through magical buildings (Clerics/Necrolytes, Conjurers/Warlocks); unit upgrades through specialized buildings; food cap management via Farm construction limiting army size
progression.unlock_mechanism: Campaign missions unlock sequentially; advanced buildings require assistance from prerequisite structures; spells unlock through magical building research
replayability_hooks: Random map generator for multiplayer and non-campaign skirmishes
Two distinct campaigns (Human and Orc) with faction-specific narratives and unit abilities
Multiplayer contests via modem or local network
Spawn installation creation for custom scenarios

world_building_techniques: Faction-based campaign narratives (Human defenders vs. Orc invaders)
Character-driven objectives (e.g., mission to kill Orc chief's daughter)
Medieval fantasy setting with magic and monsters
Asymmetric unit/spell design reflecting cultural differences (Cleric healing vs. Necrolyte skeleton raising)

faction_indicators: Human vs. Orc dichotomy with narrative roles (defenders vs. invaders)
Distinct unit aesthetics and spell identities per faction
Asymmetric magical abilities (protective Cleric vs. aggressive Necrolyte)
danger_signals: Wild monsters can be converted to player control, creating unpredictable threats
Limited resource exhaustion creating scarcity tensions
Real-time multiplayer competition with no pause
environmental_storytelling: Forests serve dual purpose as resources and early-game defensive walls
Burnt/destroyed building remnants showing previous combat
Besieged towns requiring rescue and rebuilding
Enemy camps holding captive friendly forces
